reasoning: To achieve a 1% increase from a baseline rating of approximately 2742 (standing as of late 2025), Shakhriyar Mamedyarov would need to gain roughly 27.4 Elo points by early February 2026. Because super-GMs have a K-factor of 10, gaining 27.4 points requires scoring +2.74 points *above* mathematical expectation. Over this 3-month span (November to February), elite chess players typically participate in at most one or two classical tournaments (such as Tata Steel in January). Even if he plays an active schedule of 10-15 classical games, exceeding expected score by nearly 3 whole points would demand a spectacular, rare, tournament-winning score (such as +5 or +6 against elite fields). Mamedyarov, although historically capable of brilliant streaks, is now a veteran player whose rating has generally stabilized or trended slightly downward from his peak, making such a sudden and massive jump exceedingly unlikely. Combined with the baseline chance that he plays very few or zero rated classical games during this period, the probability of him fulfilling this 1% jump condition is very low.
